name: Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges Loop
description: Cycle the difficult 29.5-mile Small loop of Saint-Gaudens, featuring Pyrenean views and 1609 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Touring cycling around Montréjeau offers diverse landscapes at the foot of the Pyrenees, featuring rolling hills and river valleys. The region is characterized by the Garonne River, which provides a dedicated cycle route, and the scenic River Ourse. Cyclists can also explore the tranquil Canal de Franquevielle and enjoy views of Lake Montréjeau. The terrain includes both gentle, paved sections and challenging ascents into the Pyrenean foothills. Best touring cycling routes around Montréjeau The most popular touring cycling route is Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges Loop, a 29.5 miles (47.5 km) trail that takes 2 hours 58 minutes to complete. This route passes through historic villages and offers views of the Pyrenees. Another top favourite among local touring cyclists is Route With View of the Valley – Saint-Béat Old Town loop from Montréjeau - Gourdan-Polignan, a difficult 45.5 miles (73.3 km) path. This route features varied terrain along the Garonne River valley and through charming old towns. Local touring cyclists also love the Route With View of the Valley – Peyresourde Pass loop from Montréjeau - Gourdan-Polignan, a 68.2 miles (109.8 km) trail leading through Pyrenean panoramas, often completed in about 7 hours 8 minutes. Touring cycling around Montréjeau is defined by Pyrenean panoramas, the Garonne River valley, and historic villages. The network offers options for different ability levels, from easy riverside paths to challenging mountain ascents. The routes in Montréjeau are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.7 stars from more than 100 reviews. More than 1,300 touring cyclists have used komoot to explore Montréjeau's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many touring cycling routes are available around Montréjeau?
A: Montréjeau offers a wide selection of touring cycling routes, with over 130 options available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including 18 easy, 65 moderate, and 50 difficult trails, ensuring there's something for every cyclist.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on touring cycling routes in Montréjeau?
A: The terrain around Montréjeau is highly diverse, ranging from gentle, paved sections along river valleys to challenging ascents into the Pyrenean foothills. You can expect routes following the &lt;strong&gt;Garonne River valley&lt;/strong&gt;, tranquil paths along the &lt;strong&gt;Canal de Franquevielle&lt;/strong&gt;, and scenic rides offering breathtaking views of the &lt;strong&gt;Pyrenees&lt;/strong&gt;.

Q: Are there touring cycling routes suitable for beginners or families in Montréjeau?
A: Yes, Montréjeau has 18 easy touring cycling routes perfect for beginners or families. The &lt;strong&gt;Canal de Franquevielle&lt;/strong&gt;, near the Cardeilhac Arboretum, offers an ideal, calm route for family cycling, often with wildlife sightings. These routes typically feature mostly paved surfaces and gentle gradients.

Q: Are there challenging touring cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. For experienced cyclists seeking a challenge, there are 50 difficult touring cycling routes. These often involve significant elevation gains and lead through Pyrenean panoramas. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41098098" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route With View of the Valley – Col des Ares loop from Montréjeau - Gourdan-Polignan&lt;/a&gt;, which features substantial climbs.

Q: What natural features or landmarks can I see along the touring cycling routes?
A: Many routes showcase the region's natural beauty. You can cycle along the &lt;strong&gt;Garonne River&lt;/strong&gt;, enjoy views of &lt;strong&gt;Lake Montréjeau&lt;/strong&gt;, or follow the peaceful &lt;strong&gt;River Ourse&lt;/strong&gt;. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1816694" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Franquevielle Canal from the Cardeilhac Arboretum&lt;/a&gt; is another notable natural feature you might encounter.

Q: Are there any historic sites or villages to explore on a touring cycling trip?
A: Yes, the region is rich in history. Many routes pass through or near historically significant towns and villages. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/15015925" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges loop from Montréjeau - Gourdan-Polignan&lt;/a&gt;, for instance, takes you near the impressive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5091783" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Cathedral of Saint Bertrand de Comminges&lt;/a&gt;. The &lt;strong&gt;Château de Valmirande&lt;/strong&gt;, a neo-Renaissance castle with a remarkable garden, is also a significant cultural stop in the vicinity.

Q: What do other touring cyclists enjoy the most about touring cycling in Montréjeau?
A: The touring cycling routes around Montréjeau are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.7 stars from over 100 reviews. Cyclists often praise the diverse landscapes, from river valleys to Pyrenean views, and the variety of routes catering to all ability levels. The opportunity to explore historic villages and natural features like the Garonne River is also frequently highlighted.

Q: Are there circular touring cycling routes around Montréjeau?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes in Montréjeau are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. An example is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/41090795" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Route With View of the Valley – Peyresourde Pass loop from Montréjeau - Gourdan-Polignan&lt;/a&gt;, offering a comprehensive tour of the Pyrenean panoramas.

Q: What is the best time of year for touring cycling in Montréjeau?
A: The region is generally appealing for touring cycling from spring through autumn. Summer offers warm weather, ideal for enjoying the lakes and rivers, though some mountain ascents can be challenging in peak heat. Spring and autumn provide milder temperatures and beautiful scenery, making them excellent choices for exploring the diverse landscapes.

Q: Where can I find parking for touring cycling routes near Montréjeau?
A: Montréjeau and its surrounding villages often provide public parking areas suitable for accessing touring cycling routes. Specific parking availability can vary by starting point, so it's advisable to check local signage or maps for designated parking zones near your chosen route's trailhead.

Q: Are there cafes or places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Yes, many touring cycling routes pass through charming villages and towns where you can find cafes, bakeries, and small shops for refreshments. Montréjeau itself features a 19th-century market hall, and other historic villages like Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges offer opportunities for a break and a bite to eat.
tips: One of the most beautiful villages in France. After a steep climb, the road goes around the village.
A stretch of road so pleasant to ride. To be included in a bucolic itinerary.
A rough coast to get there. A nice site to bivouac.
This pretty little lake in the Pyrenean foothills bears the name of the eponymous town which adjoins it. It is located on the heights of the Garonne, not far from its confluence with the Pique.

faqs.question: What is the difficulty level of the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens?
faqs.answer: This route is classified as a "Hard bike ride" and requires a very good level of fitness. It's designed for experienced touring cyclists, not beginners, and may include segments where you might need to push your bike.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on this cycling loop?
faqs.answer: The Small loop of Saint-Gaudens primarily features mostly paved surfaces. You'll encounter rolling roads winding between hillsides, characteristic of the Comminges region.

faqs.question: Are there any notable sights or viewpoints along the route?
faqs.answer: Yes, the loop offers picturesque scenery and passes by several points of interest. You can enjoy a Route With View of the Valley, the Cliffs of Troubat, and the serene Lake Saint-Pé-d'Ardet. The route also takes you near the historic settlement of Saint-Bertrand-de-Comminges.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: The research does not specify a best time of year. However, given it's a cycling route in Occitania, spring and autumn generally offer pleasant temperatures for cycling, avoiding the heat of summer or potential winter conditions.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens?
faqs.answer: The research does not specify a dedicated parking area for this exact loop. However, as it's a loop starting and ending near Saint-Gaudens, you would typically find parking within or on the outskirts of the town, or at one of the villages along the route, to begin your ride.

faqs.question: Are dogs allowed on the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens?
faqs.answer: The research does not provide specific information regarding whether dogs are allowed on this particular cycling route. Generally, dogs are permitted on public roads and paths in France, but it's always best to keep them on a leash, especially when cycling.

faqs.question: Do I need any permits or pay an entrance fee to cycle this route?
faqs.answer: No, there is no mention of any permits or entrance fees required to cycle the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens. It appears to be a publicly accessible route.

faqs.question: Does this route connect with other cycling trails?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route intersects with several other official trails. You will find sections that overlap with the Parcours Cyclable de la Garonne, De Gourdan à Saint-Pé-d'Ardet, Pedals d'Occitània, and the Véloroute du Piémont Pyrénéen 03 and 04, among others.

faqs.question: Is the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens suitable for families or beginners?
faqs.answer: No, this route is officially classified as a "Hard bike ride" and requires a "very good level of fitness." It is not recommended for families with young children or beginner cyclists.

faqs.question: What kind of scenery can I expect while cycling?
faqs.answer: You can expect picturesque scenery with rolling roads winding between hillsides. The region offers breathtaking panoramas of the Pyrenees and often follows river valleys, passing by historic towns, châteaux, and manors, providing a rich touring experience.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Small loop of Saint-Gaudens?
faqs.answer: The estimated completion time for this 47.5 km loop with 490m of elevation gain is around 2 hours and 59 minutes for an experienced touring cyclist.

waypoints.name: Route With View of the Valley
